conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to understand a bit more about Alex's current situation.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in school or college anymore. They finished their education and aren't doing any training courses at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me ask another important question:

Is Alex currently working and earning income? If so, do you know roughly how much they earn or how many hours they work per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is working. They have a full-time job at a local shop - working pretty much full-time hours during the week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

First, are you currently responsible for Alex - meaning do they live with you and do you support them financially (even partially)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home, and I do support them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about your own circumstances:

Are you currently living in the UK, and do you have the right to claim benefits in the UK (for example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status or another qualifying immigration status)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England - and I have the right to claim benefits here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

Do you receive Child Benefit for Alex currently, or have you claimed it in the past for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't currently receive Child Benefit for Alex, and I haven't claimed it before. That's why I'm asking whether I'm eligible.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Child Benefit is available for children under 16, for young people aged 16 or 17, they must be in full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels or equivalent) or approved training to qualify. Since Alex is 17 and has finished their education and is working full-time instead of being in school, college, or approved training, they don't meet the qualifying conditions.

Child Benefit typically stops when a young person either:
- Turns 16 and leaves education, or
- Turns 20 (if they continued in qualifying education or training)

Since Alex is working full-time rather than being in education or training, you wouldn't be able to claim Child Benefit for them at this time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about this decision?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Alex is working full-time at a local shop. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can claim for a child aged 16 or 17 only if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished their education and is not in any training, and is instead working full-time, they do not meet the qualifying conditions for Child Benefit. Child Benefit eligibility for 16-17 year olds requires them to be in education or approved training, which Alex is not.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with them. While the claimant meets the residency and right to claim requirements, Alex does not qualify as an eligible child because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Alex is working full-time instead. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Alex is working full-time at a local shop. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can claim for a child aged 16 or 17 only if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished their education and is not in any training, and is instead working full-time, they do not meet the qualifying conditions for Child Benefit. Child Benefit eligibility for 16-17 year olds requires them to be in education or approved training, which Alex is not.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with them. While the claimant meets the residency and right to claim requirements, Alex does not qualify as an eligible child because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Alex is working full-time instead. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
